paragraphs.text: that has all the combined romance of the ages in its history. It is rarely visited and has but

, yet it has peculiar fascination for many people. The very elements seem to conspire to make the place forbidden, as it is almost invariably blows a gale of wind in the vicinity, and when one reaches
there is no bay or harbor for a craft much larger than a rowboat.
is about seven miles long by two or three in width and rises to a height of 800 to 1,000 feet in the center.

Windswept, forbidding, haunted by the spirits of storms, it does not invite inspection and appears to have been cursed by the elements, yet despite this the island was at one time the home of a numerous people,
a race that has been lost sight of

and was literally blown off, finally making it one day about noon, coming to anchor near a long specter-like tongue of land that extends out into the ocean and where the tide ran like a millrace [sic] and a heavy groundswell swept around the point from the windward side. No more forbidding outlook can be imagined. The
appears like a sand dune. The landing was a wave-swept beach that received a high swell from three points, and it was evident that skill on landing alone would make it possible.
lay off waiting for a low sea, and finally went sailing in upon one of lesser size and
jumped overboard and held on to
that the undertow and back rush attempted to claim. In this manner

He had not heard the news of the world for a year, and did not display any ambition to learn it. But he was not happy. Too many dead people, too many bones that make wind spirits blow, he said, and when he heard that

was after the remains of these dead people he showed an inclination to leave. In all probability there is not in any land a more remarkable mound that can be seen. About 10 feet in height and nearly a mile in length, it was at this time a veritable Golgotha, a collection of bones and implements of

, ever changing, ever shifting; today exposing numbers of skeletons, tomorrow covering them up in sheets of sand. One could easily imagine that a battle had been fought here and the dead had been buried by the drifting sand, but this was not the case.

The mound was a town site upon which hundreds of people had lived for untold ages. They had brought in their shells and fishbones, piled them up at the doors of their huts, slowly building themselves up higher and higher, until in time they came to live on the the top of a vast shell mound. Here they buried their dead, concealed their household goods, and when a man or woman died his or her possessions were buried with them, and so the big mounds were made up of shells, stone implements, fish and human bones—a monument of
came from, who they were or why they lived on such
, is one of the mysteries, but it is known that the
and divided them up among the various mainland tribes. When
was being carried into execution a storm came up, and in the hurry of departure it was found that
It was impossible to land, so
She had plunged into the waves and swam a long distance to the shore, and, with
intending to return later for her.
, however, was not visited for many years.
was wrecked, but the legend lived and many years after
had survived. An expedition was go[t] up in 1850,
making a safe landing. They formed a line across
in order not to miss any signs of human habitation and began a slow search from the south point north. They soon found evidence of ancient occupation, and in a deep canyon discovered a but [hut] and
, the mother who had been deserted years before. She was living in the hut, which was made of whale ribs and [sea] lion hair, her sole companions being several wild dogs that savagely charged
could not make herself understood and, singularly enough, no one in all the Indian tribes in South
could be found who understood her.
by signs that the wild dogs had killed
long ago, that she killed sea lions and sea birds with stones and lived on them.
At first she appeared to be afraid of

, but, finding that they treated her kindly, she consented to go with them. She took all the rude articles she had used during the years of solitude, her skin dresses, made mostly from the skins of the [sea] lion, and so interesting were the costumes that they were sent to
which accomplished her rescue. She was placed in care of
who did everything possible for her.
She was bright and vivacious in disposition, learned some Spanish, danced and sang for her friends and was visited by Indians from various parts of
in an effort to find some one who could understand her.

But civilization proved too much for her and she died in three months, one of the most remarkable Crusoes known, as her story is based on actual fact and in its details is much stronger stranger than fiction.
was formed during past centuries by
, and possesses an intense interest on this account.

near the landing was 20 or 30 feet higher than the shore and reached through singular wind-worn passes, wind being the sculptor here. Once on the heights a broad mesa was seen as level as a floor and marked with lines which resembled ancient plowing or something of the kind. The mesa was covered with fine flinty stones about the size of peas, which
says were picked up by the wind and blown through the air at times. They were arraigned [arranged] like chaff on a beach.
followed thi[s] mesa for several miles, and that
had had a large and vigorous population at some distant time was evident.

Every short distance some object was found, now a pipe made from the vertebrae of a fish, a needle of fishbone, a dish made from a shell, the holes plugged with asphaltum. Here would be a stone club or mortar of steatite, a spearhead or a sinker and shell hooks that some fisherman centuries ago, perhaps, had dropped and forgotten.
Everywhere piles of big pearly shells of a size unknown here were found, telling of the lives and work of

. One mound was found covered by the ribs of whales and beneath them a flat rock bearing marks of various kinds and then the owner, a skeleton in perfect preservation, and all about it in the sand vessels of stone, flutes of bird bones and the various objects which went to make up the home of t[h]is unknown. In one spot six layers of skeletons were found, the lowest apparently very old. The question of age is always very interesting, but no one in contemplating these great shell deposits can form an adequate idea of their age.
evidently has been inhabited since the earliest times. It was doubtless fertile then, but the elements have conspired against it and it is doomed to be swept and covered with sand.
is now frequented by the professional collectors, who go over from

and live here for weeks and months, camping out on the sand dunes, weighting their tents down with rocks to prevent being blown into the sea. Every morning they go out with their hooks and poles, hunting for the spoils which have been uncovered by the wind in the night. In this way tons of material have been collected here and sent all over the world. Nearly all the great museums have collections from

. The most conspicuous objects are the stone mortars, stone vessels weighing from 100 to 200 pounds. In walking over the large mounds numbers would be found broken in pieces by some vandal or some enemy in the long ago. That these ancient people were fond of ornaments is shown by the quanitity of beads found.
